title: Using dehumidifier condensate water in the garden
subtitle: What dehumidifier water contains, which plants it is safe for, and how to store and use it
excerpt: Dehumidifier condensate is distilled water with trace contaminants from the coil, the plastic housing, and any biological growth in the drip tray. It is not suitable for drinking or cooking but is generally safe for watering ornamental plants and established trees and shrubs. This guide covers the content analysis, safe uses, and what to avoid.
sourceNotes: Water Research Centre guidance on distilled water composition. RHS advice on watering plants with non-mains water. US EPA guidance on dehumidifier condensate reuse (noting UK regulatory difference). UK Environment Agency guidance on non-mains water for household use.

glossaryTerms.definition: Water from which most dissolved minerals have been removed by evaporation and condensation. Dehumidifier condensate is effectively distilled water. It has very low mineral content and slightly acidic pH (5.5 to 6.5), unlike mains water in hard-water areas which is alkaline.

A measure of the combined content of all inorganic and organic substances dissolved in water. Mains water in a hard-water area: 300 to 500 ppm. Dehumidifier condensate: typically 10 to 50 ppm. Very low TDS water does not provide plant nutrients and may leach minerals from the soil.

content.text: A dehumidifier running 4 hours per day in a 40 m² basement extracts 2 to 5 litres of

per day in summer. Over a month that is 60 to 150 litres. Using it in the garden rather than discarding it saves mains water and has some benefit in hard-water areas where alkaline mains water can raise soil pH over time.
Condensate is primarily distilled water with

typically below 50 ppm. Trace contaminants may include copper or aluminium from an old coil, trace organic compounds from the plastic housing, and biological content if the drip tray grows mould. A clean, well-serviced dehumidifier gives very low contamination. A dirty drip tray or mouldy coil raises the biological content.
Watering ornamental plants, shrubs, and trees: safe. Low mineral content is not a problem for established plants in soil.

Watering acid-loving plants (blueberries, rhododendrons, azaleas) in hard-water areas: beneficial. The low pH of condensate (5.5 to 6.5) matches the preferred soil pH for these species and avoids the alkalinity of hard mains water.
Topping up water butts: safe. Dilutes the collected rainwater and extends the water butt supply.
Edible crops where water contacts the edible part: do not use condensate on leafy salads, herbs, or soft fruit where the water touches what you eat. Use on the soil around the root zone only.
Container plants with no soil reservoir: low TDS water used exclusively may slowly leach nutrients from potting mix. Mix with rainwater or supplement potting mix nutrients regularly.
Drinking or cooking: do not use condensate for food or drink. It is not safe to drink.

Condensate stored more than 2 to 3 days develops bacterial growth, particularly in warm weather. Use promptly or add a few drops of ordinary household bleach per litre if storing for garden use. Do not store in the dehumidifier collection tank for more than 48 hours: empty the tank daily.

The drip tray or collection tank has biological growth. Clean the tray with a dilute bleach solution, rinse well, and clean the dehumidifier coil with a soft brush. Condensate from a clean dehumidifier should be odourless.
